item.title: How tough does an investment banking boss need to be?
item.description: The difference between good and bad leaders turns on whether they recognise when pressure is productive

item.title: The shrinking of Jewish life in Britain
item.description: It should be entirely possible to be anti-Israel without sinking to dehumanising language

item.title: The irresistible rise of authorial AI
item.description: Humans must get used to living and working with the quirks of machine-written text

item.title: Palantir’s latest hit softwear: a $239 chore coat
item.description: Who cares about taste when billions of dollars are being bandied about?

item.title: Big airline bosses’ confidence should trouble their investors
item.description: Carriers do not seem to be baking in slowing demand

item.title: My latest portfolio revealed
item.description: Now the Bank of England has called the top, I’ve never been so bullish about stocks

item.title: Must we really ‘do lunch’?
item.description: Yes we’re late to this. But no one has solved it yet, so here goes

item.title: US GDP: still ultimately about the consumer
item.description: Plus central bank hold ’em

item.title: TK Elevator sale shows private equity’s elevator pitch still works
item.description: The only way is up for Cinven and Advent after their deal with Finnish lift maker Kone

item.title: How bots could help revive democracy
item.description: New AI tools encouraging deliberation could promote consensus and reduce polarisation in politics

item.title: Has taste in music been hijacked?
item.description: Digital marketers admit to flooding sites with fake comments to promote acts, posing a disturbing threat to media integrity

item.title: From Lady Chatterley to Marilyn to Dua Lipa, books are in rude health
item.description: Reading for both pleasure and betterment endures — even if unquestioning faith in literary canons and critical authority is gone

item.title: Alexander Calder’s sculptures offer much for the gardener’s eye
item.description: The artist’s work with movement, space — and time — has inspired three recent exhibitions, and a newly planted memorial

item.title: Jay Powell’s defiant curtain call
item.description: Kevin Warsh still needs to prove that he can stand up to the US president

item.title: Big Tech’s AI payback might be coming into view
item.description: Earnings hint at growth trajectory despite increases in capital spending

item.title: Disney’s skirmish with Trump’s TV watchdog is no joke
item.description: The media group cannot easily afford to ditch its profitable linear networks, which limits its ability to defy the FCC

item.title: In praise of tech troublemakers
item.description: As CEOs refuse constraints, workers feel a responsibility to prevent AI’s most dangerous uses

item.title: How the Trump-Xi threats of trade war softened into a quieter rivalry
item.description: The forthcoming US-China leaders’ summit will be much less fraught than the last one

item.title: The problematic inevitability of Andy Burnham
item.description: Labour’s assumptions about its leader-in-waiting are logical but no guarantee of success

item.title: Welcome our new robotaxi overlords
item.description: The arrival of driverless vehicles is a second chance for cities

item.title: How to help left-behind places
item.description: Better data, industrial policy and education could all ameliorate inequalities

item.title: Can’t cook, won’t cook? Start by feeding yourself
item.description: A cure for kitchen nerves, plus a recipe for anchovy pasta

item.title: The energy crisis is no excuse for bad subsidies
item.description: We know what worked and what failed the last time around

item.title: The Fed’s balancing act
item.description: Plus tech earnings

item.title: UAExit adds momentum to the Opec doom loop
item.description: The Emirates’ departure reflects the cartel’s declining market share and the ongoing energy transition
